perturbation.original_text: Force Majeure means the occurrence of any acts of God or man-made disasters or accidents during the term of this Agreement which is unforeseeable or, if foreseeable, unavoidable, or uncontrollable and make it impossible for one Party to perform this Agreement in a whole, including earthquakes, typhoons, floods, fires, wars, strikes, riots, hacker attacks, technical breakdown of telecommunication departments, and legal restrictions.
perturbation.changed_text: Force Majeure means the occurrence of any acts of God, limited to earthquakes, typhoons, and floods, that make it impossible for one Party to perform this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowly defining 'Force Majeure' to only include specific natural disasters, the contract excludes other events commonly considered force majeure, potentially conflicting with established legal interpretations and common law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law and Contract Interpretation Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the principle that 'force majeure' clauses are interpreted in light of the reasonable expectations of the parties and common business understandings. By drastically narrowing the definition, the contract deviates from established legal precedents regarding unforeseen circumstances excusing performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Force Majeure' by explicitly limiting it to specific natural disasters, excluding other commonly recognized events. This deviates from established legal interpretations and business expectations regarding unforeseen circumstances, as the Perturbation Explanation highlights. While the provided snippets offer no specific laws to reference, the Law Explanation supports the determination of a contradiction, stating that the modification contradicts the principle that 'force majeure' clauses are interpreted in light of reasonable expectations and common business understandings. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable. The original text included a broader range of events, making the modified text a significant and impactful change.

perturbation.original_text: Business Secrets mean any technical, financial, commercial or any other information owned and treated as business secrets by one Party and/or its subsidiaries or affiliates, which have the following attributes: (i) It is unknown to the public; (ii) It may generate economic benefit for its owner; (iii) It is practical; and (iv) It is treated as business secrets with appropriate protection measures by its owner.
perturbation.changed_text: Business Secrets mean any information, whether or not generally known or protected, that one Party deems confidential.
perturbation.explanation: This change broadens the definition of 'Business Secrets' to include information that might not traditionally qualify, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that require secrecy and reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable and is subject to efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified text removes the requirement that the information is 'unknown to the public' and whether is treated with protective measures, thus potentially including information that wouldn't be protected under trade secret law.

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the protection of 'Business Secrets' by removing the requirement that the information be 'unknown to the public' and treated with protective measures. The Law Explanation highlights that UTSA defines a trade secret as deriving economic value from not being generally known and being subject to efforts to maintain secrecy. By allowing information 'whether or not generally known or protected' to be considered a business secret, the changed text contradicts the core tenet of trade secret law, creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: PRC Laws mean any laws, regulations, rules and regulatory documents in the PRC which are current and will be issued going forward.
perturbation.changed_text: PRC Laws mean only those laws, regulations, and rules explicitly published and translated into English by the Supreme People's Court of China as of the date of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly restricts the scope of 'PRC Laws' to only include explicitly translated laws by a specific entity, excluding many regulations and interpretations crucial for legal compliance in China.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Civil Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_citation: Article 150 of the General Principles of Civil Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 150 states "The laws shall have binding force unless they are contrary to the Constitution." By only acknowledging the translated laws, it excludes the wide array of legal norms and regulations that govern business activities in China. Also, the English translated versions may not be legally binding and can be open to interpretation.

Home User Management About Us Chinese lawinfochina.com > Laws & Regulations > Text Bookmark Download Print Search: "Fabao" Window Font Size: The Decision of the State Council on Amending the Regulation of the People's Republic of China on Safeguard Measures [Effective] 国务院关于修改《中华人民共和国保障措施条例》的决定(2004) [现行有效] 【法宝引证码】 Issuing authority： State Council Document Number： Order No. 403 of the State Council of the People's Republic of China Date issued： 03-31-2004 Effective date: 06-01-2004 Level of Authority: Administrative Regulations Area of Law: Foreign Economy and Trade Order of the State Council of the People's Republic of China (No. 403) The Decision of the State Council on Amending the Regulation of the People's Republic of China on Safeguard Measures is hereby promulgated and shall be implemented as of June 1, 2004. Wen Jiabao Premier of the PRC March 31, 2004 The Decision of the State Council on Amending the Regulation of the People's Republic of China on Safeguard Measures The State Council decides to make the following amendments to the Regulation of the People's Republic of China on Safeguard Measures: 中华人民共和国国务院令 （第403号） 现公布《国务院关于修改〈中华人民共和国保障措施条例〉的决定》，自2004年6月1日起施行。 总理 温家宝 二00四年三月三十一日 国务院关于修改《中华人民共和国保障措施条例》的决定 国务院决定对《 中华人民共和国保障措施条例 》作如下修改： 1. “The Ministry of Foreign Trade and Economic Cooperation (MOFTEC)” as mentioned in Articles 3, 4, 5, 9, 14, 18, 21, 22, 25, 30 and 35 shall be amended as the “Ministry of Commerce (MOFCOM)”. 一、 将第三条、第四条、第五条、第九条、第十四条、第十八条、第二十一条、第二十二条、第二十五条、第三十条、第三十三条中的“对外贸易经济合作部（外经贸部）”修改为“商务部”。 2. Article 6 shall be amended as “The investigation and determination of damage shall be made by the MOFCOM, and the investigation of the damage of agricultural products for taking safeguard measures shall be made by the MOFCOM jointly with the Ministry of Agriculture”. ．．．．．． 二、 将第六条修改为：“对进口产品数量增加及损害的调查和确定，由商务部负责；其中，涉及农产品的保障措施国内产业损害调查，由商务部会同农业部进行。” ．．．．．． Dear visitor,you are attempting to view a subscription-based section of lawinfochina.com. perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'PRC Laws,' limiting it to only explicitly translated laws published by the Supreme People's Court. This contradicts the intent of encompassing all relevant laws and regulations necessary for compliance in China. The original text includes 'any laws, regulations, rules and regulatory documents in the PRC which are current and will be issued going forward', which is comprehensive. The snippet provided shows the State Council amending regulations, indicating the broad scope of enforceable rules beyond just translated laws from the Supreme People's Court. The changed text's exclusion of untranslated laws and regulations creates a legal contradiction because it would allow a party to argue non-compliance based on regulations not translated by the specified entity, undermining the obligation to adhere to all PRC Laws.
